sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 17-year-old non-binary minor, presents with a newly diagnosed elevated cholesterol level of 210, which was detected during an annual checkup.
The patient expresses concern and confusion about the result, as their grandparents reported not having similar issues at a young age.
The patient has a history of being active, riding a bicycle frequently, and consuming a diet that includes some high-fat foods, such as chocolate chip cookies.
The patient has a good relationship with their grandparents, who reported no family history of hypercholesterolemia.
The patient has been living on their own for some time and does not have any smoking exposure at home or with friends.
Past medical history: The patient has no significant past medical history, but did have a borderline high cholesterol level last year that was not considered a concern at the time.
Review of systems: The patient reports feeling healthy and has been working on making health goals, including feeling more non-gendered.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure was found to be normal during the physical exam.
Physical exam: The physical exam revealed no abnormalities, including no murmur, rub, or gallop on the heart exam, clear lungs, no thyroid enlargement, and no hepatomegaly or splenomegaly.
Test Results: The patient's cholesterol level was elevated at 210.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has elevated cholesterol, likely due to a combination of genetic and lifestyle factors, which requires further investigation and management.
Problem list: Hypercholesterolemia; Suspected familial hypercholesterolemia; [NOT MENTIONED]
Plan:
I ordered a full cholesterol panel, glucose, complete metabolic panel, thyroid studies, and liver panels to investigate the cause of the elevated cholesterol.
I also ordered genetic testing to determine if the high cholesterol is familial.
I advised the patient to follow a well-balanced diet, including foods lower in saturated fat and sugars, and to meet with a nutritionist to discuss dietary changes.
I instructed the patient to continue being active and exercising, specifically bike riding, and to follow up in 3-6 months with another cholesterol level check after implementing dietary changes.
Follow-up: I will follow up with the patient in 3-6 months to check their cholesterol level after they have met with the nutritionist and implemented dietary changes.
